topic: structure of water and hydrogen bonding
explain: Polarity is an unequal distribution of charge across a molecule. In water, oxygen is slightly negative and the hydrogens are slightly positive.
bullet_points: Water is polar because oxygen pulls electrons more strongly.
Partial charges allow water molecules to interact strongly.

topic: structure of water and hydrogen bonding
explain: A hydrogen bond is a weak attraction between a partially positive hydrogen atom and a partially negative atom on another molecule or another part of a molecule.
bullet_points: It is weaker than a covalent bond.
It forms because of partial charges.
Hydrogen bonds are common between water molecules.

topic: structure of water and hydrogen bonding
text: Why is water able to form hydrogen bonds with other water molecules?
explain: Water can form hydrogen bonds because it is polar. The partially positive hydrogens of one water molecule are attracted to the partially negative oxygen of another.
bullet_points: Hydrogen bonds form between neighboring water molecules.

topic: structure of water and hydrogen bonding
explain: Cohesion is the attraction between molecules of the same substance. In water, hydrogen bonding causes water molecules to stick to each other.
bullet_points: In water, caused by hydrogen bonds.
Helps water move in continuous columns through plants.

topic: structure of water and hydrogen bonding
explain: Adhesion is the attraction between molecules of different substances. Water can stick to other polar surfaces, such as the walls of xylem tissue.
bullet_points: Water adheres to other polar materials.

topic: structure of water and hydrogen bonding
explain: Surface tension is the resistance of a liquid surface to being broken. In water, it results from strong cohesion among water molecules at the surface.
bullet_points: Caused by cohesion among water molecules.
Makes the surface behave like a thin film.
Depends on hydrogen bonding in water.

topic: structure of water and hydrogen bonding
explain: Capillary action is the upward movement of water through narrow spaces because of both cohesion between water molecules and adhesion between water and another surface.
bullet_points: Occurs in narrow tubes or spaces.
Helps water move through plant tissues.

topic: structure of water and hydrogen bonding
text: Why does water have a high specific heat?
explain: Water has a high specific heat because hydrogen bonds absorb energy before the water molecules speed up enough to change temperature greatly.
bullet_points: Hydrogen bonds must absorb energy first.
Helps stabilize temperatures in organisms and environments.

topic: structure of water and hydrogen bonding
text: Why does water have a high heat of vaporization?
explain: Water has a high heat of vaporization because many hydrogen bonds must be broken for water molecules to evaporate.
bullet_points: This takes a large amount of energy.
Helps cooling by evaporation, such as sweating.

topic: structure of water and hydrogen bonding
text: Why is ice less dense than liquid water?
explain: Ice is less dense than liquid water because hydrogen bonds hold water molecules in a more spread-out lattice when water freezes.
bullet_points: Frozen water forms an open structure.
Molecules are farther apart in ice.
That lower density allows ice to float.

text: What are the four major elements that make up most living matter?
explain: The four major elements are carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, and nitrogen. Together they make up most of the mass of biological molecules.
bullet_points: These elements make up most biological matter.

explain: Trace elements are elements required by organisms in very small amounts. Even though they are needed only in tiny quantities, they can still be essential for life.

text: Why is carbon especially important in biology?
explain: Carbon is especially important because it can form four covalent bonds, allowing it to build stable chains, rings, and complex organic molecules.
bullet_points: It can bond to many different atoms, including itself.

text: Why is nitrogen important in living organisms?
explain: Nitrogen is important because it is a component of amino acids and nucleotides. Organisms need it to build proteins and nucleic acids.

text: Why is phosphorus important in living organisms?
explain: Phosphorus is important because it is part of nucleotides, ATP, and phospholipids. It helps cells store energy and build membranes and nucleic acids.

text: Why is sulfur important in living organisms?
explain: Sulfur is important because it is present in some amino acids and helps stabilize the structure of certain proteins through disulfide bonds.
bullet_points: Found in some amino acids, such as cysteine.

explain: A monomer is a small molecular subunit that can be joined to other similar subunits to build a larger molecule.
bullet_points: Used to build larger biological molecules.

explain: A polymer is a large molecule made of many repeating subunits called monomers.
bullet_points: Examples include proteins and nucleic acids.

explain: A macromolecule is a large biological molecule. In AP Biology, the major macromolecule groups are carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids.
bullet_points: Four major groups are carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids.
Many are built from smaller subunits.

explain: Dehydration synthesis is a reaction that joins smaller molecules together by removing a water molecule.
bullet_points: Builds larger molecules from smaller ones.

explain: Hydrolysis is a reaction that breaks a larger molecule into smaller parts by adding water.
A carbohydrate is a biological molecule made mainly of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen that functions in energy storage, structure, or cell recognition.
bullet_points: Can be used for energy or structure.

explain: A monosaccharide is a single sugar unit and is the monomer of many carbohydrates.
A disaccharide is a carbohydrate made of two monosaccharides joined by a glycosidic linkage.
A polysaccharide is a carbohydrate made of many monosaccharides linked together.
bullet_points: Can serve storage or structural roles.
Examples include starch, glycogen, cellulose, and chitin.

explain: Starch is a polysaccharide used by plants to store glucose for later use.
bullet_points: Can be broken down for energy.

explain: Glycogen is a highly branched polysaccharide used by animals to store glucose.
bullet_points: Stored mainly in liver and muscle cells.

explain: Cellulose is a structural polysaccharide in plant cell walls. Its glucose subunits are linked in a way that gives plant tissues strength.
bullet_points: Humans cannot digest its beta linkages.

explain: Chitin is a structural polysaccharide found in fungal cell walls and in the exoskeletons of arthropods.
A glycosidic linkage is the covalent bond that joins monosaccharides together in a carbohydrate.
A lipid is a mostly nonpolar biological molecule used in long-term energy storage, membranes, signaling, or waterproofing.
bullet_points: Functions in energy storage, membranes, and signaling.

explain: A triglyceride is a lipid made of one glycerol molecule and three fatty acids. It is a major form of long-term energy storage.
bullet_points: One glycerol plus three fatty acids.

explain: A fatty acid is a hydrocarbon chain with a carboxyl group. Fatty acids are components of triglycerides and phospholipids.

text: What is the difference between saturated and unsaturated fatty acids?
explain: Saturated fatty acids have no carbon-carbon double bonds, while unsaturated fatty acids have one or more double bonds that create bends in the chain.
bullet_points: Unsaturated = one or more double bonds.
Unsaturated fatty acids usually increase membrane fluidity.

explain: A phospholipid is a lipid with a glycerol backbone, two fatty acids, and a phosphate-containing head. Phospholipids are the main molecules in cell membranes.
Amphipathic means having both a hydrophilic region and a hydrophobic region in the same molecule.
bullet_points: Has both water-attracting and water-repelling parts.

explain: A steroid is a lipid made of four fused carbon rings. Steroids can function as hormones or as structural molecules such as cholesterol.
Cholesterol is a steroid that helps maintain membrane stability and fluidity in animal cells.
bullet_points: A membrane component in animal cells.

explain: A nucleic acid is a biological molecule that stores or transmits genetic information. DNA and RNA are the two main nucleic acids.
bullet_points: DNA and RNA are nucleic acids.

explain: A nucleotide is the monomer of nucleic acids. It contains a sugar, a phosphate group, and a nitrogenous base.
bullet_points: Links together to form nucleic acids.

explain: The sugar-phosphate backbone is the repeating chain of sugars and phosphates that forms the structural side of a nucleic acid strand.
bullet_points: Made of alternating sugars and phosphates.
Forms the outside of DNA or RNA strands.

explain: Complementary base pairing is the specific pairing of nitrogenous bases. In DNA, adenine pairs with thymine and guanine pairs with cytosine.
bullet_points: A pairs with T in DNA and U in RNA.

explain: A purine is a nitrogenous base with a double-ring structure. Adenine and guanine are purines.
A pyrimidine is a nitrogenous base with a single-ring structure. Cytosine, thymine, and uracil are pyrimidines.
bullet_points: Cytosine, thymine, and uracil are pyrimidines.

explain: A protein is a polymer of amino acids that performs structural, transport, signaling, movement, defense, or enzymatic functions.
An amino acid is the monomer of a protein. Each amino acid has an amino group, a carboxyl group, a hydrogen, and an R group attached to a central carbon.
bullet_points: Has amino group and carboxyl group.
The R group differs among amino acids.

explain: A peptide bond is the covalent bond that joins amino acids together in a polypeptide.
A polypeptide is a chain of amino acids joined by peptide bonds. One or more polypeptides can make up a functional protein.
bullet_points: May fold into a functional protein.

text: What is primary structure in a protein?
explain: Primary structure is the linear sequence of amino acids in a polypeptide.
bullet_points: Determines higher levels of protein structure.

text: What is secondary structure in a protein?
explain: Secondary structure is local folding of a polypeptide into shapes such as alpha helices and beta pleated sheets due to hydrogen bonding in the backbone.
bullet_points: Includes alpha helices and beta sheets.

text: What is tertiary structure in a protein?
explain: Tertiary structure is the overall three-dimensional shape of a single polypeptide formed by interactions among side chains.

text: What is quaternary structure in a protein?
explain: Quaternary structure is the arrangement of multiple polypeptide subunits in a single functional protein.
bullet_points: Subunits interact to form a functional protein.
Not all proteins have quaternary structure.

explain: Denaturation is the loss of a protein’s normal shape, often caused by changes in temperature, pH, or salt concentration, which can reduce or destroy function.
bullet_points: Can be caused by heat or pH changes.

explain: An enzyme is a biological catalyst, usually a protein, that speeds up a reaction by lowering activation energy.
bullet_points: Speeds reactions by lowering activation energy.
